commonProblems.h2: Common BMW Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: BMW’s modern engine range — particularly the N47, N57, and B-series families — has developed a reputation for specific, predictable failure points that UK owners search for daily. While BMW acknowledged timing chain weaknesses in Technical Service Bulletins (TSB SI B11 02 17 for B47 diesels, SI B11 01 18 for B48 petrols), many owners find themselves outside extended warranty coverage facing repair bills that rival the car's value . Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the four most common BMW engine failures in the UK, what they cost to fix at a dealer versus a specialist, and when replacement is the smarter financial decision.

problems.h4: Timing Chain Failure – Why N47 & Early B-Series Diesel Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.rootCause: The N47 engine's timing chain is located at the rear of the engine block, running through an oil passage. The original single-row chain stretches and the plastic tensioner guides degrade over time. When the chain snaps, pistons collide with valves — catastrophic engine destruction requiring full replacement. BMW acknowledged the issue in internal TIS bulletin SI B 11 03 10 but never issued a formal recall . Pre-2018 B47 and B48 units suffer similar chain tensioner wear, confirmed in BMW service bulletins SI B11 02 17 and SI B11 01 18 .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Full timing chain kit (chain, tensioner, guides, sprockets), oil pump chain replacement, sump clean | Extends engine life 60,000–80,000 miles.
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable BEFORE chain slips or rattles on cold start exceeds 3 seconds

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt / Reconditioned N47 Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of professionally rebuilt engine with upgraded timing components (metal tensioner, revised guides), rebuilt turbo, decoked EGR, full compression/leakdown testing | 90,000+ mile post-rebuild lifespan with proper maintenance. Recommended if chain already slipped or valves bent

problems.recommendation: If your N47 rattles only briefly on cold start (under 3 seconds) but compression and cam timing are still good, preventative chain replacement is the most cost‑effective route — specialist indie pricing saves you ~40% vs dealer. However, if the chain has skipped timing or the engine already shows low compression, skip the repair. A reconditioned engine from a specialist is only £600–£1,700 more than dealer chain replacement and includes a 12‑month warranty plus upgraded components that permanently address the original design flaw.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt N47 engine prices for your 1 Series, 3 Series or 5 Series

problems.h4: B48 / B58 Coolant System Failure – Why Modern Petrols Lose Coolant & Overheat
problems.affectedModels: BMW 2 Series (220i, 230i, 2014–2020, B48B20), 3 Series G20 (320i, 330i, 2019–present), 4 Series G22 (420i, 430i), 5 Series G30 (520i, 530i), X3 G01, X4 G02, X5 G05 (B58B30), Z4 G29, Toyota Supra.
problems.rootCause: The B48 and B58 “modular” petrol engines use a plastic coolant expansion tank and hoses that become brittle with heat cycling. Cracks develop at the tank's seam and hose connectors, leading to gradual or sudden coolant loss. If ignored, overheating warps the cylinder head and blows the head gasket — a repair often exceeding £3,000 .

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Hose or Expansion Tank Replacement
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of cracked expansion tank, split hose, or loose clamp. Refill and bleed cooling system | OEM-grade parts last 5+ years.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if caught early with no overheating history

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace electric water pump (B58 uses mechanical pump), thermostat, coolant flush and refill | Restores cooling system to factory specification. Recommended if pump fails or engine runs hot despite no visible leaks

| Cylinder head removal, pressure testing, head gasket replacement, bolts, coolant system overhaul | Only viable if head not warped. If overheating caused head deformation, replacement is often cheaper
| Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with new gaskets, timing components, and upgraded cooling system parts | Required if head warped, block cracked, or bearing damage occurred from overheating

problems.recommendation: Check your coolant level monthly — B48/B58 owners who catch a leak early fix it for under £200. If your engine has overheated to the point of steam or warning lights, have a compression test done immediately. On a car worth under £8,000, a full dealer head gasket repair (£2,500+) rarely makes sense — specialist replacement or a reconditioned engine is often the same money for a complete solution. For M240i, M340i, and M440i owners, the reconditioned B58 route preserves performance value better than a patch repair.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt B48 and B58 engines with upgraded cooling system components

problems.h4: S55 Crank Hub Spinning – The M3 / M4 Performance Engine Weakness
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 20,000–60,000 miles (more common on tuned or track-driven cars)
problems.rootCause: The S55's crankshaft hub is held in place only by friction from a single central bolt, not a physical key or spline. Under high torque or sudden load changes (aggressive downshifts, launch control), the hub can slip relative to the crankshaft — this “spun crank hub” throws off cam and crank timing. If severe, pistons hit valves, engine destroyed. Less than 1% of stock S55s fail, but modified cars see higher rates .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Dealer resets timing, replaces hub bolt, reassembles. Does NOT install upgraded hub | 30,000–50,000 mile risk of recurrence.
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable if hub slipped minimally without valve contact

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of fully rebuilt S55 with upgraded crank hub, new bearings, rings, gaskets | Required if valves bent or block damaged. Less than 1% of spun hubs reach this stage

problems.recommendation: If you own a stock S55 with no symptoms, you don't need preventative work — actual failure rates are under 1% . However, if you tune your car, track it regularly, or plan to keep it beyond warranty, the £3,200–£4,500 spline-lock hub upgrade is sensible insurance. If your hub has already slipped but timing is still intact, pay the £1,500 specialist labour for a re-time PLUS the upgraded hub — don't let a dealer just reinstall the factory setup. On a £30,000–50,000 M car, the preventative spend is worth peace of mind.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt S55 engine prices and crank hub upgrade packages

problems.group: N63 V8 Valve Stem Seal Failure
problems.h4: N63 V8 Valve Stem Seal Failure – X5, 5 Series & 7 Series Oil Consumption
problems.rootCause: The N63 “hot-vee” V8 routes the exhaust and turbos inside the engine's V-angle, generating extreme heat around the valve stems. The original Viton rubber seals harden and shrink, allowing oil to leak down valve guides into combustion chambers. Blue smoke on cold start, followed by constant smoking and rapid oil consumption (1 litre per 600 miles not uncommon) .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove cylinder heads, replace all 32 valve stem seals with upgraded Viton/Glyd rings (high-temp rated). New valve cover gaskets | 60,000–80,000 mile lifespan with proper oil changes.
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if engine otherwise healthy and compression good

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of fully rebuilt engine with upgraded valve seals, new timing chains, rebuilt turbos, updated injectors | Permanent solution. Often cheaper than dealer seal replacement + any other simultaneous repairs

problems.recommendation: On an N63-powered X5 or 550i worth £7,000–12,000, the £1,700–2,500 specialist seal replacement is the smart play — it halves dealer pricing and restores the engine for years. Only go full replacement if compression tests show additional wear (rings, scored bores) or the turbos are failing. Owner forum data suggests many N63s with seal replacement done at 70,000 miles exceed 130,000 miles without recurrence . Use a BMW specialist who has done this job before — the “hot-vee” layout demands specific tooling and procedure.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for N63 valve stem seal repair or rebuilt N63 engines

types.title: BMW Reconditioned Engines (Recon)

types.title: BMW Rebuilt Engines

types.title: Used BMW Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: BMW Refurbished Engines

engineSizes.h2: BMW Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every BMW engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: BMW 1.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre three-cylinder diesel appeared in the BMW 1 Series (F70) from 2024 onwards, producing 136–150hp. This B37-family engine represents BMW's modular small diesel platform, designed for efficiency and urban driving. Replacement demand remains low due to its recent introduction, but early reports focus on DPF regeneration issues in primarily short-trip use
items.cta: Get quotes for BMW 1.5L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: DPF clogging in low-mileage usage – prevention requires regular longer runs

items.title: BMW 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: BMW’s 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel has powered more UK BMWs than any other engine. The M47 (1998–2007) was the first-generation common-rail diesel, followed by the N47 (2007–2015) — infamous for rear-mounted timing chain failure that can destroy the engine without warning. The B47 (2014–present) largely resolved chain issues but introduced EGR cooler failures and occasional injector problems. This engine family appears across the 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Series, X1, X3, and Mini Cooper D
items.cta: Get quotes for BMW 2.0L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: B47 – EGR cooler failure requiring replacement

items.title: BMW 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: BMW’s 3.0-litre straight-six diesel is the gold standard for torque and refinement. The M57 (1998–2009) earned a reputation for bulletproof reliability, powering E39 530d and E60 530d. Its successor, the N57 (2008–2019), introduced twin-turbo variants (N57D30T0) producing up to 381hp in the X5 M50d, but suffered EGR cooler leaks (risking hydro-lock) and turbo actuator failure around 80,000 miles. The B57 (2015–present) returns to the M57's robust design with fewer major failure reports, though some 2015–2018 units experienced timing chain tensioner wear
items.cta: Get quotes for BMW 3.0L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: N57 – EGR cooler leaks and turbo actuator failure
B57 – early timing chain tensioner wear (pre-2018)

items.title: BMW 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2-litre three-cylinder B38 powers entry-level BMW models and the Mini range. Producing 102–136hp, it's found in the 1 Series (F20/F40) and X1 (F48). Replacement is relatively rare as these engines are modern and generally reliable, though the most common failure is the high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) around 60,000–80,000 miles
items.cta: Compare BMW 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre three-cylinder B38 (also branded as the Mini Cooper engine) delivers 136–170hp. It appears in the 1 Series (F40), 2 Series Gran Coupe (F44), X1 (U11), and X2. This is the same modular engine as the 1.2L but with a longer stroke. Known issues are minimal, though the engine management system (DME) can suffer software-related cold-start roughness
items.cta: Compare BMW 1.5L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: DME software glitches (cold-start roughness) – requires ECU update

items.title: BMW 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6-litre four-cylinder Prince engine (co-developed with PSA) appeared in the 1 Series (E81/E87) and Mini from 2006–2015. The N13 (2011–2015) turbocharged version produced 136–170hp. Replacement demand is moderate due to age – common failures include timing chain guide wear (N13), valve cover gasket leaks, and VANOS solenoid failure
items.cta: Compare BMW 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8-litre four-cylinder appeared across multiple generations. Early M40/M42/M43 engines powered E30 318i (1987–1995) and E36 318i (1991–1998). The N42/N46 (2001–2007) introduced Valvetronic — a common failure point when the valvetronic eccentric shaft motor fails (£1,200–£1,800 repair). By 2007, BMW phased out 1.8L petrol in favour of the 2.0L B48 in various states of tune
items.cta: Compare BMW 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: BMW’s 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol engine has evolved through five generations. The M50B20 (1990–1996, 150hp) was BMW's first modern DOHC four-cylinder, but VANOS seal failure is common. The N46B20 (2004–2007, 143hp) suffered valvetronic eccentric shaft motor failure. The N20 (2011–2017, 184–245hp) brought turbocharging — and timing chain guide vulnerability (the "N20 death rattle"). The current B48B20 (2015–present, 184–258hp) is the modular replacement, though early B48 units (2015–2018) can suffer coolant leaks from the expansion tank and HPFP failure at 60,000+ miles
items.cta: Compare BMW 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5-litre straight-six was once BMW’s entry-level six-cylinder. The M50B25 (E34 525i, 1991–1995) and M54B25 (E46 325i, 2000–2005) are both robust, though cooling system failures (water pump, expansion tank) are common across all older straight-six engines — overheating leads to head gasket failure and, if ignored, full engine replacement
items.cta: Compare BMW 2.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: BMW’s naturally aspirated 3.0-litre straight-six — the M54B30 (E46 330i, 2000–2006), N52B30 (2004–2015), and N53B30 (direct injection, 2007–2013) — are considered some of BMW's most reliable engines when maintained. The N53's direct injectors (piezo type) are expensive to replace when they fail (£400–£600 each, six required). The turbocharged N54 (2006–2016, 335i) — BMW's first mass-produced turbo petrol — suffers wastegate rattle, HPFP failure, and carbon build-up on intake valves . The later N55 (2009–present) resolved many N54 issues but can still experience VANOS bolt failure (pre-2012) and oil filter housing gasket leaks. The B58 (2015–present) is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern six-cylinder, though 2015–2018 units had coolant leak issues from the expansion tank and occasional HPFP failures
items.cta: Compare BMW 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 4.0L–4.4L V8 Petrol Engines
items.description: BMW's V8 petrol engines range from the early M60 (1992–1995, 282hp) and M62 (1996–2003, 282–342hp) to the N62 (2001–2010, 325–362hp) with Valvetronic, and the N63 (2008–present, 400–523hp) "hot-vee" twin-turbo design. The M60 and early M62 suffer from Nikasil cylinder lining corrosion due to high-sulphur fuels (less common now). The N62 and N63 are notorious for valve stem seal failure (blue smoke on start-up, £1,700–£2,500 repair), with N63 also experiencing timing chain wear and injector failure. The current S68 (2022–present, M5/XM) and N63TU3 show improved reliability
items.cta: Compare BMW 4.0–4.4L V8 petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 4.4L–4.8L V8 Petrol Engines (Performance Variants)
items.description: High-output variants of BMW's V8 include the 4.4L N63TU (550hp in X5 M50i), S63 (550–600hp in M5/M6/X5M/X6M), and the naturally aspirated 4.8L N62B48 (362hp in 550i). The S63 adds twin-scroll turbos and upgraded cooling, but shares the N63's susceptibility to valve stem seal failure (though at lower rates). Replacement costs for S63 units are high: a rebuilt S63 with upgraded seals is £7,000–£12,000 fitted
items.cta: Compare BMW 4.4–4.8L V8 petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: Hot-vee heat management – valve seal failure

items.title: BMW 5.0L V10 Petrol Engine (S85)
items.description: The S85B50 is BMW's only production V10 — a 5.0-litre naturally aspirated engine producing 507hp in the E60 M5 and E63 M6. It revs to 8,250rpm and uses individual throttle bodies. Replacement demand exists due to the rod bearing failure epidemic — original bearings wear prematurely (often requiring replacement every 60,000–80,000 miles), and failure seizes the engine completely. A rebuilt S85 with upgraded bearings costs £10,000–£15,000
items.cta: Compare BMW 5.0L V10 petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: Rod bearing failure (pre-emptive replacement recommended every 60k miles)

items.title: BMW 6.0L–6.75L V12 Petrol Engines
items.description: BMW's V12 engines powered the 7 Series and Rolls-Royce models. The M70 (1988–1994, 5.0L, 300hp) was BMW's first post-war V12, but it's complex and parts are becoming scarce. The later N73 (2003–2016, 6.0L, 438hp in 760i) and N74 (2009–2022, 6.0L–6.75L, 535–601hp) are more common in the UK. Replacement outside of warranty is financially challenging due to parts availability and labour (engine-out for many repairs). Most owners facing V12 failure in older 7 Series (£5,000–8,000 market value) choose to scrap rather than replace
items.cta: Compare BMW V12 petrol engine prices

items.title: BMW 2.0L Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)
items.description: BMW's plug-in hybrid system pairs the 2.0-litre B48B20 petrol engine with an electric motor integrated into the ZF 8-speed automatic transmission. Total system output ranges from 215hp (330e) to 292hp (530e xDrive). The combined unit is known as the "eDrive" system. Replacement is specialist-only — the hybrid system requires high-voltage training and BMW ISTA diagnostics . Battery degradation (capacity loss after 8–10 years) is the most common failure, not the combustion engine itself

engineCodeDirectory.h2: BMW Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major BMW engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK BMW specialists.

entries.title: N47D20 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Engine The N47D20 powered over a million UK BMWs including the 1 Series (E87/F20 116d/118d/120d), 3 Series (E90/F30 318d/320d), 5 Series (F10 520d), X1 (E84 sDrive18d), X3 (E83/F25 xDrive20d), and Z4 (E89 sDrive20d) from 2007 to 2015. The engine's fatal flaw is the rear-mounted timing chain – BMW placed the chain at the flywheel end, making replacement a 12+ hour engine-out job. The plastic chain guides disintegrate, the chain stretches, and when it snaps (typically 60,000–100,000 miles), pistons strike valves causing catastrophic engine destruction. A quality rebuilt N47 includes a complete timing chain kit with revised metal-backed guides, new oil pump chain, rebuilt turbo, decoked EGR, and full compression/leakdown testing.
entries.cta: Get quotes for N47 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: N57D30 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Engine The N57D30 is BMW's 3.0-litre straight-six diesel fitted to the 3 Series (E90/F30 330d/335d), 5 Series (E60/F10 530d/535d), 7 Series (F01 730d), X3 (F25 xDrive30d), X5 (E70/F15 xDrive30d/xDrive40d), X6, and the triple-turbo X5 M50d (N57D30T0, 381hp) from 2008 to 2019. Two failures dominate replacement demand: EGR cooler leakage (coolant enters cylinders, risks hydro-lock) and turbo actuator failure (wastegate seizes, boost loss). Rebuilt N57 engines include a new EGR cooler, rebuilt turbos with upgraded actuators, new timing components, and a full set of injectors.

entries.title: M57D30 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Engine The M57D30 – BMW's "bulletproof" straight-six diesel – powered the E46 330d, E39/E60 530d, E53 X5 3.0d, and E83 X3 3.0d from 1998 to 2009. Unlike its N57 successor, the M57 has no major catastrophic failure patterns. Replacement demand exists due to age (high mileage, worn injectors, tired turbos) rather than design flaws. A rebuilt M57 includes new injector seals, rebuilt turbo, timing chain kit, and full gasket set.
entries.cta: Get quotes for M57 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: B47D20 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Engine The B47D20 replaced the N47 from 2014 onward, appearing in the 1 Series (F20/F40 116d/118d/120d), 3 Series (F30/G20 318d/320d), 5 Series (G30 520d), X1 (F48 sDrive18d), X3 (G01 xDrive20d), X4 (G02), X5 (G05), and Mini Cooper D. BMW relocated the timing chain to the front and revised the tensioner – largely solving N47's catastrophic failures. However, pre-2018 B47 units suffer EGR cooler failure: coolant leaks into the exhaust, and if the cooler cracks internally, coolant can enter cylinders causing hydro-lock. Replacement B47 engines from our network include an upgraded EGR cooler, new timing chain, and revised injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for B47 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: B57D30 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Engine The B57D30 is BMW's current-generation 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (2015–present), fitted to the 3 Series (G20 330d), 5 Series (G30 530d/540d), 7 Series (G11 730d), X3 (G01 xDrive30d), X5 (G05 xDrive30d/xDrive40d), X6, X7, and the quad-turbo X5 M50d (B57D30T0, 400hp). Early B57 engines (2015–2018) experienced timing chain tensioner wear resulting in cold-start rattle. Unlike the N57, EGR cooler failure is less common. Rebuilt B57 units include revised tensioners, new chains, and a full injector and turbo inspection.

entries.title: N54B30 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Twin-Turbo Engine The N54B30 – BMW's first mass-production turbo petrol engine – powered the 1 Series (E82 135i), 3 Series (E90/E92 335i), 5 Series (E60 535i), X6 (E71 xDrive35i), and Z4 (E89 sDrive35i) from 2006 to 2016. The N54 earned a reputation for three expensive failure modes: high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure (£650–£950, leaves the car undriveable), wastegate rattle (requiring turbo replacement), and carbon build-up on intake valves (requiring walnut blasting every 50k–80k miles). A quality rebuilt N54 includes a new HPFP, rebuilt turbos with upgraded wastegates, decarbonised cylinder head, new injectors, and revised PCV system.
entries.cta: Get quotes for N54 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: N55B30 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Single-Turbo Engine The N55B30 replaced the N54 from 2009 onward, fitting the 1 Series (M135i F20), 2 Series (M235i F22), 3 Series (F30 335i), 4 Series (435i), 5 Series (F10 535i), X3 (F25 xDrive35i), X5 (F15 xDrive35i), X6, and M2 (N55B30T0). BMW addressed HPFP and wastegate issues, but two problems remain: VANOS bolt failure (pre-2012 engines – bolts shear, causing timing errors) and oil filter housing gasket (OFHG) leaks (oil leaks onto the serpentine belt, which can slip and get pulled into the crank seal – catastrophic engine failure). Rebuilt N55 engines include upgraded VANOS bolts, new OFHG, timing chain kit, and rebuilt turbo.

entries.title: N20B20 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Turbo Engine The N20B20 (2011–2017) powered the 1 Series (118i/120i), 3 Series (F30 320i/328i), 5 Series (F10 520i/528i), X1 (xDrive20i), X3 (F25 xDrive20i), X4, Z4 (sDrive20i), and countless Minis. The engine became infamous for timing chain guide failure – the plastic guides break, the chain loosens, and if not caught immediately, the chain slips or snaps causing valve-piston contact. Owners report a "diesel-like rattle" on cold start as the first warning. A rebuilt N20 includes a complete timing chain kit, new oil pump chain, revised guides, VANOS units, and a full top-end rebuild.
entries.cta: Get quotes for N20 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: B58B30 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The B58B30 powers the 1 Series (M140i F20), 2 Series (M240i F22), 3 Series (G20 M340i), 4 Series (G22 M440i), 5 Series (G30 540i), X3 (G01 M40i), X4 (G02 M40i), X5 (G05 xDrive40i), X6, X7, Z4 (G29 M40i), and Toyota Supra from 2015 onward. Widely considered BMW's most reliable modern six-cylinder, replacement demand is low. The only recurring issue on early (2015–2018) B58 units is the cooling system: the plastic coolant expansion tank and hoses become brittle and crack, leading to coolant loss and potential overheating. Rebuilt B58 engines include upgraded coolant system components (aluminium tank where available), new timing chain, and full diagnostic testing.

entries.title: B48B20 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The B48B20 – BMW's modular 2.0-litre four-cylinder turbo (2015–present) – fits the 1 Series (118i/120i), 2 Series (218i/220i), 3 Series (G20 320i/330i), 4 Series (420i/430i), 5 Series (520i/530i), X1, X2, X3 (xDrive20i/xDrive30e), X4, Z4 (sDrive20i), and Minis. The B48 is generally reliable, but early units (2015–2018) suffer: high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure (£650–£950, common across all BMW direct-injection engines) and coolant leaks from the expansion tank. Rebuilt B48 engines include a new HPFP, upgraded coolant system components, timing chain kit, and injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for B48 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: S55B30 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Twin-Turbo Engine The S55B30 powers the F80 M3 and F82/F83 M4 (2014–2020). The engine itself is robust, but a design flaw makes it vulnerable: the crankshaft hub is held in place by friction only (no keyway or spline). On tuned cars or aggressive track use, the hub can spin relative to the crank – "spun crank hub" – throwing off cam timing, bending valves, and destroying the engine. Stock cars have a very low failure rate (<1%), but each failure is catastrophic. A rebuilt S55 includes a spline-lock crank hub upgrade (VTT or similar), new bearings, timing chain, and all gaskets. Dealership replacement cost is £18,000–£22,000; our specialist network quotes £10,000–£14,000 for a rebuilt unit.

entries.title: S63B44 4.4L V8 Petrol
entries.description: Twin-Turbo Engine The S63B44 powers the F10/F90 M5, F06 M6, F85 X5M, and F86 X6M from 2010 onward. As a high-performance version of the N63 "hot-vee" V8, it shares the same weakness: heat degrades valve stem seals, causing blue smoke on cold start and rapid oil consumption. The S63 also experiences turbo coolant line leaks (the lines run through the engine's V-angle, exposed to extreme heat) and timing chain stretch. A rebuilt S63 includes upgraded valve stem seals (high-temperature Viton), new coolant lines, rebuilt turbos, timing chain kit, and high-flow injectors.
entries.cta: Get quotes for S63 BMW engine replacement

entries.title: N63B44 4.4L V8 Petrol
entries.description: Twin-Turbo Engine The N63B44 (2008–present) appears in the 5 Series (F10 550i), 7 Series (F01 750i), X5 (E70/F15 xDrive50i), and X6 (E71 xDrive50i). The "hot-vee" design places the exhaust and turbos inside the engine's V-angle, generating extreme heat around the valve guides. The original valve stem seals harden and shrink, allowing oil to leak into combustion chambers – blue smoke on start-up, eventually constant smoking and oil consumption of 1 litre per 600 miles. A rebuilt N63 includes upgraded high-temperature valve seals, new timing chains, rebuilt turbos, and new injectors. Specialist seal replacement costs £1,700–£2,500; full rebuilt engine supply and fit is £7,000–£10,000.

entries.title: S85B50 5.0L V10 Petrol
entries.description: Engine The S85B50 – BMW's only production V10 – powered the E60 M5 and E63 M6 (2005–2010). The engine is a masterpiece (8,250 rpm redline, individual throttle bodies) but has one catastrophic weakness: rod bearing failure. The original bearings wear prematurely (often needing replacement every 60,000–80,000 miles), and when they fail, the crankshaft seizes, destroying the engine. A rebuilt S85 includes upgraded bearing shells (Clevite or ACL), new connecting rods, crankshaft polish, and all gaskets. Replacement cost from our network is £10,000–£15,000 – dealership prices exceed £25,000.
entries.cta: Get quotes for S85 BMW engine replacement

faq.h2: BMW Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: ** Quick, honest answers to the most common questions BMW owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a BMW engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt BMW engine typically costs between £2,000 and £5,500 for common 2.0‑litre diesel units like the N47 and B47, while performance engines such as the S55 (M3/M4) or N63 V8 range from £7,000 to £12,000 fitted. Reconditioned units with upgraded components (timing chain kits, revised EGR coolers) cost 20–30% more than a used take‑out engine but include up to 24 months warranty. Fitting adds £400–£800 depending on the model – front‑wheel‑drive cars like the 1 Series (F40) are slightly faster, while X5 engine swaps require more labour. The price you see online often excludes delivery and VAT, so always request a full breakdown.
items.cta: Compare BMW engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a BMW engine replacement?
items.answer: Four factors drive the price: engine size and rarity (a B58 straight‑six costs more than a B47 four‑cylinder), condition (rebuilt with warranty vs used as‑is), your vehicle's age (older E90 3‑Series engines are cheaper and faster to source than G20 units), and whether you choose supply‑only (£400–£800 less) or full supply‑and‑fit. Location also matters – labour rates in London and the South East are typically 20–30% higher than the North or Scotland. Our marketplace shows you itemised quotes from multiple specialists so you can see exactly what you're paying for, including delivery, fitting, and warranty.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK BMW engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my BMW?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and bodywork are in good condition. A professionally rebuilt N47 or N57 diesel fitted for £2,500–£4,000 will often outlast the original unit because our network uses upgraded timing chains, revised EGR coolers, and new turbochargers. The alternative – selling the car as a non‑runner (typically £500–£1,500) and buying another used BMW – carries its own risks (unknown service history, hidden failures). On a 320d worth £4,000–£8,000, replacement is almost always the smarter financial decision. On a high‑mileage 1 Series worth under £2,000, replacement rarely makes sense.
items.cta: Find reconditioned BMW engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: What's included in a BMW engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit package includes sourcing a quality rebuilt or reconditioned engine matched to your exact engine code (e.g., N47D20C for a 2013 320d), removing the failed unit, transferring your ancillaries (injectors, turbo, manifolds), installing the replacement with new gaskets, oil, filters, and coolant, and a test drive to confirm everything works. The service also includes disposal of the old engine (often exchange basis) and a workmanship warranty (minimum 12 months). Fitting alone typically adds £400–£800 to the engine price – less for like‑for‑like swaps on common models like the 3 Series, more for X5/X6 or V8 models requiring subframe removal.
items.cta: Compare BMW engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a BMW engine replacement take?
items.answer: On common models like the 3 Series (F30 320d) or 1 Series (F20 118d), a like‑for‑like engine swap takes 3–5 working days once the specialist has the replacement unit. More complex models – X5 (E70/F15), 5 Series with V8, or M cars – can take 5–7 days because of additional disassembly and parts transfer time. The biggest variable is engine availability: our network stocks commonly replaced codes (N47, N57, N20, B48) for next‑day dispatch, but rarer codes (S85 V10, M57TU2) may take 5–10 days to source. Always ask your chosen supplier for their current lead time before committing.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see which specialists can fit your BMW fastest

items.question: Do reconditioned BMW engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every engine supplied through our network carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many specialists offer extended 24‑month coverage on rebuilt and remanufactured units at an additional cost (typically 10–15% of the engine price). The warranty covers major internal components: crankshaft, connecting rods, pistons, piston rings, cylinder head, valves, camshafts, timing chain, oil pump, and gaskets. Wear items (belts, hoses, spark plugs, filters) and external bolt‑ons (turbo, injectors, alternator) are usually covered for 3–6 months only – confirm exactly what's included with your supplier. A warranty is your best protection against unexpected failure.
items.cta: Get quotes for BMW engines with warranty included

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned BMW engine?
items.answer: A used (take‑out) engine is the cheapest option – typically £1,200–£2,500 for common N47 units – but it's an unknown quantity. It may have the same underlying failure (e.g., timing chain stretch) waiting to happen. A reconditioned engine (£2,200–£4,500 for a 2.0‑litre diesel) has been stripped, cleaned, inspected, and fitted with new components such as timing chains, gaskets, piston rings, and bearings. For failure‑prone BMW engines – N47 (timing chain), N57 (EGR cooler), N20 (timing chain guides) – we strongly recommend reconditioned with upgraded parts. A used engine makes sense only if you plan to sell the car within 12 months or the vehicle has little residual value.

items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK‑wide network of vetted BMW engine specialists, from independent garages in the West Midlands (a historic hub for BMW diesel rebuilders) to dedicated performance shops in Surrey and Essex handling M‑car engine swaps. Our registration lookup instantly matches your specific engine code – N47, B58, S63, etc. – to nearby suppliers who have that unit in stock and offer fitting. No need to search "BMW specialist near me" across multiple sites. Nationwide delivery is also available if you prefer to use your own local garage for fitting.
items.cta: Compare quotes from BMW engine specialists across the UK

items.question: What are the most common BMW engine failures?
items.answer: The most common catastrophic failures driving BMW engine replacement in the UK are: **N47/N47D20 2.0‑litre diesel** – timing chain stretch and guide failure (2007–2015, any 116d/118d/120d/316d/318d/320d). **N57 3.0‑litre diesel** – EGR cooler leaks causing hydro‑lock and turbo actuator failure (2008–2019, 330d/530d/X5 30d). **N20 2.0‑litre petrol** – timing chain guide failure (2011–2017, 520i/328i/X1/X3). **N63 4.4‑litre V8 petrol** – valve stem seal failure causing blue smoke and oil consumption (2008–2015, 550i/750i/X5 50i). **S55 3.0‑litre twin‑turbo** – crank hub spin on tuned/tracked M3/M4 cars. In all these cases, early detection rarely prevents the engine from needing full replacement – a rebuilt unit with upgraded components is the solution.
items.cta: Get quotes for a replacement engine designed to fix your BMW's known failure

items.question: How can I get the best price on a BMW engine?
items.answer: Always compare at least three quotes – prices for the same N47 engine can vary by £1,000+ depending on the supplier's location, warranty length, and whether they have the unit in stock. Choose a used engine if your budget is under £2,000 and you only need the car to last 12–18 months. Choose a reconditioned engine with upgraded components (new timing chain, revised EGR cooler, rebuilt turbo) if you plan to keep the BMW for 3+ years – the upfront premium pays for itself in reliability. Ask potential suppliers whether their price includes delivery, fitting, VAT, and the old engine exchange – hidden extras can add 30% to the headline figure. Use our comparison tool to see all‑inclusive quotes side by side.
